summary: The author achieved 2nd place in an Akkadian-to-English machine translation competition by treating data construction as the primary engineering challenge rather than focusing on model architecture. They implemented a three-stage LLM pipeline to generate clean sentence-level alignments from official training data and corrupt metadata, and independently mined ~60 external academic PDFs to expand the corpus. A custom text normalization script unified highly variable cuneiform orthography, while document-level chunking and multi-version LLM extraction provided contextual signal and implicit augmentation. The final submission relied on a vanilla ByT5-large model trained on ~90,522 curated pairs, demonstrating that systematic data curation and scale outweighed architectural modifications or post-processing tricks.
extensive_summary:
## Data-Centric Approach & LLM Alignment Pipeline
The competition presented a severe data bottleneck, with only ~6,500 full-document pairs and ~13,456 published texts, many lacking sentence-level alignment or containing corrupt metadata. The author treated data construction as the primary engineering challenge, implementing a three-stage LLM pipeline to partition the official data into non-overlapping sets: **Breaker** (segmenting clean full-text translations into sentence pairs using OARE API word indices as anchors), **Fixer** (re-anchoring corrupt S_meta fragments using normalized word forms and physical line numbers), and **Generator** (attempting to synthesize translations from morphological metadata, which was ultimately discarded due to noise).

## External Data Mining & Augmentation
The single largest score improvement came from independently mining ~60 academic publications on Old Assyrian cuneiform from journals and open-access repositories. These PDFs were OCR-processed and extracted, preserving their inherent sentence-level structure. To handle multilingual sources (Turkish, German, French), the extraction pipeline directly translated them into English, expanding the corpus to ~60,654 external sentence pairs. The author implemented a prefer-EN deduplication strategy to avoid conflicting translations for the same tablet, and applied a 2× boost to English academic papers to balance exposure against naturally amplified Turkish sources. Multi-version extraction (three separate LLM passes per non-English source) provided implicit data augmentation through translation variance.

## Text Normalization & Training Configuration
Akkadian transliteration orthography is highly inconsistent, so a custom normalization script unified surface variants (e.g., `sz`/`s` → `š`, subscript vowels to acute/grave accents, deleting hamza variants, standardizing determinatives). Training utilized a vanilla `google/byt5-large` model with a max sequence length of 768 bytes. Consecutive sentences were concatenated into document-level chunks up to the 768-byte ceiling to provide inter-sentence context and match the model's exact input capacity. The author experimented with Adafactor optimizer settings, discovering that setting `β₁=0.9` stabilized training when using `group_by_length=True`, which otherwise caused non-uniform gradient magnitudes due to length-bucketed batching. Ultimately, the submitted solution relied on ~90,522 clean sentence pairs and standard hyperparameters, proving that systematic data curation and scale outweighed architectural modifications or post-processing tricks.
competition_flow: Raw official CSVs and external academic PDFs were processed through a three-stage LLM alignment pipeline and OCR extraction, normalized for orthographic consistency, chunked into document-level sequences, and trained on a vanilla ByT5-large model with Adafactor to produce the final translation submission.
data_reading: CSV files (train.csv, published_texts.csv, S_meta) parsed via standard tools; academic PDFs OCR-processed and extracted via an LLM-based pipeline; custom dataset generator handles multi-version backups and deduplication.
data_processing: Three-stage LLM pipeline for sentence alignment (Breaker, Fixer, Generator); OCR extraction of ~60 external academic PDFs; custom text normalization unifying highly variable cuneiform orthography (e.g., sz/s→š, subscript vowels to accents, hamza deletion); prefer-EN deduplication to prevent conflicting translations; 2× boosting of English academic papers; multi-version LLM extraction passes for implicit augmentation; concatenation of consecutive sentences into 768-byte document-level chunks; group_by_length batching to minimize padding waste.
ensembling: No ensembling was used; a fuzzy-matching post-processor for proper names was attempted but discarded due to lack of improvement.
insights: Treating data construction as the primary engineering challenge outweighs architectural modifications for severely data-constrained NMT tasks.
A widening public/private leaderboard gap signals the need for more diverse external data rather than hyperparameter tuning.
Using group_by_length batching introduces non-uniform gradient magnitudes that can be stabilized by enabling the first-moment estimate (β₁=0.9) in Adafactor.
Post-hoc name normalization is unnecessary if the model is trained on sufficient, correctly aligned source data.
critical_findings: Synthesizing pseudo-translations from morphological metadata alone produced too much noise and actively degraded model performance.
Adding word-level dictionary translation pairs as training samples provided zero benefit despite intuitive appeal.
Running the LLM extraction pipeline three separate times on multilingual sources yields slightly different English renditions that serve as effective implicit data augmentation.
Sources already covered by the official alignment pipeline should be excluded from multi-version extraction to avoid generating near-duplicates that add no new information.
what_did_not_work: LLM-generated pseudo-translations from morphological metadata alone were too noisy and hurt the score.
Word-level dictionary augmentation pairs showed no benefit.
Fuzzy-matching PN/GN post-processor failed to improve scores due to inconsistent name conventions across diverse test sources.
